claim: Free-roaming farm dogs have full home ranges of 2.72–3.38 km² with core areas of 0.54–0.89 km², demonstrating that dogs with open-access behavior remain geographically constrained under normal conditions.
stance_target: movement ecology baseline: normal dog ranging vs. displaced/panicked dog travel distances
scope_conditions: n=3 working farm dogs, UK; GPS + accelerometer tracking; pilot study
quoted_support: Dog 3 had the smallest core and full range size (0.54 km² and 2.72 km² respectively) compared to dog 1 (0.89 km² and 3.38 km² respectively) and dog 2 (0.77 km² and 3.06 km² respectively).

claim: Farm dogs spent 60–71% of their time within 25m of field boundaries, suggesting that free-roaming dogs preferentially follow structural landscape features — relevant to predicting lost dog movement near roads and fences.
stance_target: movement corridor hypothesis for lost dog search planning
scope_conditions: n=3 farm dogs, UK; GPS tracking across farm terrain with defined boundary features
quoted_support: The dogs were frequently located within close proximity to the field boundaries (71, 70 and 60% of time across the study duration for dogs 1, 2 and 3 respectively).
numbers: 60-71% of time within 25m of field boundaries

claim: Individual dogs show highly variable day-to-day spatial overlap (Bhattacharyya coefficient 0.09–0.94), indicating that dog ranging patterns are not consistent and are influenced by temporal and situational factors rather than fixed routes.
stance_target: predictability of lost dog movement patterns
scope_conditions: n=3 farm dogs, UK; individual-level variation
quoted_support: Dog 1 shows the least overlap in space utilization between days, with a mean BC of 0.27 (range of BC = 0.09 to 0.63) compared to dog 2 (mean BC of 0.67; range of BC = 0.13 to 0.94).
